title: Please Stop Making Me Opt Out of AI
gist: Meta's decision to auto-enable an AI feature letting users generate images using public Instagram accounts' likenesses (opt-out by default) triggered swift public backlash and a rollback within three days, spotlighting an industry-wide pattern of opt-out-by-default AI and privacy settings that experts say needs GDPR-style default protections or federal legislation.

insights.claim: Meta enabled an AI image-generation feature using public Instagram accounts' likenesses on an opt-out basis, and after three days of viral creator backlash (one Instagram video explaining how to opt out drew over 3 million views), Meta rolled the feature back, calling it something that 'missed the mark.'
insights.speaker: Reece Rogers
insights.so_what: Demonstrates that consumer and creator backlash against non-consensual AI likeness features can force a reversal within days, a fast-acting reputational risk for any platform shipping similar opt-out AI features.

insights.claim: EFF senior privacy activist Thorin Klosowski says the roughly three-day turnaround from launch to rollback of Meta's Instagram AI-tagging feature was unusually fast for a shipped generative AI product, calling it 'some kind of record.'
insights.speaker: Reece Rogers
insights.so_what: Signals AI-likeness and deepfake-adjacent features carry outsized, fast-moving public-relations risk relative to other opt-out privacy settings.
insights.quote: That was a clear and immediate pushback. Honestly, it was great to see how quickly that happened.
insights.locator: The public reaction to Instagram's 'opt-out' default

themes.theme: Opt-out defaults as an industry-wide pattern

insights.claim: As public sentiment toward generative AI has soured, large platforms including Meta, Google, Dropbox, and LinkedIn have responded by enabling AI features and related data-use settings on by default, requiring users to actively find and disable them rather than opt in.
insights.speaker: Reece Rogers
insights.so_what: An industry-wide behavioral pattern investors should expect to recur: default-AI-feature launches followed by user backlash and possible rollback or regulatory scrutiny.
insights.locator: As public sentiment has continued to sour

insights.claim: Boston University law professor Woodrow Hartzog notes that users overwhelmingly stick with whatever the default option is, meaning opt-out AI or data-training defaults reliably produce much higher enrollment than an opt-in design would.
insights.speaker: Reece Rogers
insights.so_what: Explains the commercial incentive for platforms to keep choosing opt-out defaults for AI features and data training despite backlash risk; default-setting choice is a direct, quantifiable lever on AI training-data volume and feature adoption.
insights.quote: People tend to stick with whatever the default option is. So, if the default option is that you're enrolled, you're probably going to stay enrolled.
insights.locator: 'People tend to stick with whatever the default option is'

insights.claim: The EU's GDPR Article 25 requires privacy-by-design so that the more privacy-protective option must be pre-selected by default, a structural mandate largely absent from current US federal law.
insights.speaker: Reece Rogers
insights.so_what: EU-style default-privacy mandates are the regulatory template most likely to be cited in future US federal legislation targeting opt-out AI and data practices.

insights.claim: Consumer Federation of America's Ben Winters argues Meta and its peers are 'stewards of the opt-out status quo' enabled by the absence of adequate US privacy regulation, and while past federal privacy legislation attempts have failed, he sees rising public sentiment moving the US closer to federal regulation than a decade ago.
insights.speaker: Reece Rogers
insights.so_what: Watch for renewed federal privacy and AI-default legislation efforts; scattered state laws such as those in California and Maryland are currently the only checks in place.
insights.quote: They are stewards of the opt-out status quo that we find ourselves in, without adequate privacy regulation in the States.
insights.locator: 'It is the perfect recipe for something that needs federal government intervention'

themes.theme: Downstream harm from default AI opt-ins

insights.claim: Woodrow Hartzog frames default AI opt-ins as consequential design choices rather than neutral tools, warning that automatically enrolling millions of users into a likeness-generation or deepfake-adjacent tool makes a world filled with even more deepfakes more likely.
insights.speaker: Reece Rogers
insights.so_what: Ties platform default-setting decisions directly to aggregate deepfake and NCII proliferation, connecting default-design choices to broader nudify-app and deepfake harm trends.
insights.quote: If a company automatically opts millions of users into a deepfake tool, then a world filled with even more deepfakes becomes more possible.
